1:
"The elders which are among you I exhort, who am also an elder, and a witness of the sufferings of Christ, and also a partaker of the glory that shall be revealed:" The witnesses note Peter's exhortation to the elders, joining himself to them. Gill notes the apostle returns to particular exhortations, after his general ones concerning patient suffering, and having exhorted subjects, servants, and husbands and wives, here addresses the elders. The witnesses note Peter exhorting the elders among them, calling himself also an elder, a witness of Christ's sufferings, and a partaker of the glory to be revealed; the apostle's humble appeal to the elders as a fellow-elder — that Peter, turning to the pastors of the churches, exhorts them not from above as a lordly apostle but as one of themselves: "the elders which are among you I exhort, who am also an elder" (placing himself alongside them in the same work) — and he names two things that fit him to exhort them: "a witness of the sufferings of Christ" (one who saw and testifies of Christ's passion) "and also a partaker of the glory that shall be revealed" (a sharer with them in the hope of the coming glory); the witnesses note the lowliness and the warmth of this appeal — Peter, though an apostle, comes as a fellow-elder, a fellow-witness of the cross, and a fellow-heir of glory, the better to win the elders to their duty; the elders which are among you I exhort, who am also an elder. The witnesses note the elders which are among you I exhort, who am also an elder. The witnesses note a witness of the sufferings of Christ, and also a partaker of the glory that shall be revealed.

The witnesses note Peter's humble appeal to the elders as a fellow-elder — a witness of Christ's sufferings and partaker of the coming glory; not lording over them but joining himself to them.

So the witnesses make the verse the apostle's appeal as a fellow-elder. The lesson the expositors draw is that Peter exhorts the pastors humbly, as one of themselves, a fellow-witness of the cross and heir of glory. The witnesses note the I exhort: "the elders which are among you I exhort" — Gill notes he turns to the pastors; the I exhort; the the elders; the the pastors; the addresses; the exhorts. The expositors observe the also an elder: "who am also an elder" — placing himself alongside them; the also an elder; the alongside; the one of themselves; the the same work; the humble. The witnesses press the witness of the sufferings: "a witness of the sufferings of Christ" — one who saw Christ's passion; the witness of the sufferings; the saw; the the passion; the testifies; the the cross. The expositors draw the lesson on the partaker of the glory: "a partaker of the glory that shall be revealed" — a sharer in the coming glory; the partaker of the glory; the sharer; the the coming glory; the the hope; the heir. The witnesses observe the lowliness: the lowliness and warmth of the appeal; the lowliness; the warmth; the the appeal; the humble; the fellow. The expositors note the to win them: the better to win the elders to their duty; the to win them; the the elders; the their duty; the fellow-elder; the appeal. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the elders which are among you I exhort who am also an elder, the a witness of the sufferings of Christ and also a partaker of the glory that shall be revealed, and the appeal as a fellow-elder. The verse thus records the apostle's appeal as a fellow-elder — teaching that Peter exhorts the pastors humbly, as one of themselves, a fellow-witness of the cross and heir of glory.
2:
"Feed the flock of God which is among you, taking the oversight thereof, not by constraint, but willingly; not for filthy lucre, but of a ready mind;" The witnesses note the charge to feed God's flock with right motives. Gill notes the phrase "the flock of God which is among you" describes the flock to be fed, distinguishing it as God's own, not the pastors' — and "feed" includes the whole work of the pastoral office. The witnesses note Peter charging the elders to feed God's flock and take the oversight, not by constraint but willingly, not for filthy lucre but of a ready mind; the pastoral duty to feed and oversee the flock from pure motives — that Peter gives the elders their great work and the spirit in which it must be done: "feed the flock of God which is among you" (nourish and shepherd the people, who are God's own flock, not their own) "taking the oversight thereof" (exercising the pastoral care and government) — and he marks the right motives by contrast with the wrong: "not by constraint, but willingly" (not as a forced, grudging task, but gladly and freely), "not for filthy lucre, but of a ready mind" (not for greed of base gain, but with an eager, devoted heart); the witnesses note that the flock is God's, the elders but under-shepherds, and the work is to be done with willing love, not reluctance or covetousness; feed the flock of God... not by constraint, but willingly; not for filthy lucre, but of a ready mind. The witnesses note feed the flock of God which is among you, taking the oversight thereof. The witnesses note not by constraint, but willingly; not for filthy lucre, but of a ready mind.

The witnesses note the charge to feed God's flock — not their own — and oversee it, not by constraint but willingly, not for greed but with a ready mind; the elders but under-shepherds.

So the witnesses make the verse the pastoral duty from pure motives. The lesson the expositors draw is that the elders are to feed and oversee God's flock willingly and freely, not from constraint or greed. The witnesses note the feed the flock: "feed the flock of God which is among you" — Gill notes nourish God's own flock; the feed the flock; the nourish; the God's own; the not their own; the shepherd. The expositors observe the oversight: "taking the oversight thereof" — exercising pastoral care and government; the oversight; the pastoral care; the government; the the office; the shepherd. The witnesses press the not by constraint: "not by constraint, but willingly" — not a forced task but gladly; the not by constraint; the not forced; the willingly; the gladly; the freely. The expositors draw the lesson on the not for lucre: "not for filthy lucre, but of a ready mind" — not for greed but with eager heart; the not for lucre; the not greed; the a ready mind; the eager; the devoted. The witnesses observe the under-shepherds: the elders are but under-shepherds of God's flock; the under-shepherds; the the elders; the God's flock; the not their own; the the Lord's. The expositors note the willing love: the work is to be done with willing love; the willing love; the not reluctance; the not covetousness; the gladly; the devoted. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the feed the flock of God which is among you taking the oversight thereof, the not by constraint but willingly not for filthy lucre but of a ready mind, and the pastoral duty. The verse thus records the pastoral duty from pure motives — teaching that the elders are to feed and oversee God's flock willingly, not from constraint or greed.
3:
"Neither as being lords over God's heritage, but being ensamples to the flock." The witnesses note the charge against lording over the flock, and to lead by example. Gill notes "God's heritage," or "clergy" (the word used of the whole people of God, his lot and inheritance), is not to be governed with tyranny, in a haughty, imperious, and arrogant manner. The witnesses note Peter charging that the elders not be lords over God's heritage, but be examples to the flock; the pastoral leadership by example, not domineering rule — that Peter completes the manner of the eldership: "neither as being lords over God's heritage" (not domineering, lording it over the people as petty tyrants, ruling by their own arbitrary will) — for the flock is "God's heritage," his own inheritance and possession, not theirs to tyrannize — "but being ensamples to the flock"; the witnesses note the better way of leading — the true pastor governs not by overbearing authority but by being a pattern, a living example of all he teaches, drawing the flock to follow by going before them in faith, holiness, and love; so that the elder leads as a shepherd and model, not as a lord; neither as being lords over God's heritage, but being ensamples to the flock. The witnesses note neither as being lords over God's heritage. The witnesses note but being ensamples to the flock.

The witnesses note the charge against domineering — not lording over God's heritage but leading by example; the true pastor a living pattern, drawing the flock by going before them.

So the witnesses make the verse the leadership by example, not domineering. The lesson the expositors draw is that the elder is not to lord it over the flock but to lead as a living example. The witnesses note the not lords: "neither as being lords over God's heritage" — Gill notes not domineering tyrants; the not lords; the not domineering; the tyrants; the arbitrary; the imperious. The expositors observe the God's heritage: the flock is God's own inheritance, not theirs to tyrannize; the God's heritage; the his inheritance; the his possession; the not theirs; the the people. The witnesses press the ensamples: "but being ensamples to the flock" — be living examples; the ensamples; the living examples; the a pattern; the model; the lead. The expositors draw the lesson on the by example: the true pastor leads by example, not authority; the by example; the not authority; the the pattern; the going before; the draw. The witnesses observe the going before: drawing the flock by going before them in faith and love; the going before; the in faith; the in holiness; the in love; the draw. The expositors note the shepherd not lord: the elder leads as a shepherd and model, not a lord; the shepherd not lord; the the shepherd; the the model; the not a lord; the lead. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the neither as being lords over God's heritage, the but being ensamples to the flock, and the leadership by example. The verse thus records the leadership by example, not domineering — teaching that the elder is not to lord it over the flock but to lead as a living example.
4:
"And when the chief Shepherd shall appear, ye shall receive a crown of glory that fadeth not away." The witnesses note the reward of the faithful under-shepherds at Christ's appearing. Gill notes by "the chief Shepherd" is meant Christ, who may well be so called, since he is God's fellow, equal with him, and the great Shepherd over all the under-shepherds; this is the encouraging motive to faithful service. The witnesses note Peter promising that when the chief Shepherd appears, they shall receive a crown of glory that fades not away; the unfading crown promised to faithful pastors at Christ's coming — that Peter sets the great reward before the faithful elders: "and when the chief Shepherd shall appear" (when Christ, the Chief Shepherd over all the pastors who are his under-shepherds, comes again in glory) "ye shall receive a crown of glory that fadeth not away"; the witnesses note the beauty of the reward and its contrast with earthly crowns — the faithful pastor shall be crowned, not with a fading garland of leaves like the victors of old, but with "a crown of glory" that never withers or fades; so that the elder who serves God's flock faithfully now, looking for no earthly gain, is encouraged by the sure and everlasting crown his Chief Shepherd will give at his appearing; ye shall receive a crown of glory that fadeth not away. The witnesses note when the chief Shepherd shall appear. The witnesses note ye shall receive a crown of glory that fadeth not away.

The witnesses note the unfading crown promised to faithful pastors — when the chief Shepherd appears, a crown of glory that never withers, unlike the fading garlands of earthly victors.

So the witnesses make the verse the crown promised to faithful pastors. The lesson the expositors draw is that the faithful elder shall receive an unfading crown of glory when Christ the Chief Shepherd appears. The witnesses note the chief Shepherd: "when the chief Shepherd shall appear" — Gill notes Christ, the Shepherd over all under-shepherds; the chief Shepherd; the Christ; the over all; the the under-shepherds; the God's fellow. The expositors observe the appear: when Christ comes again in glory; the appear; the comes again; the in glory; the his coming; the revealed. The witnesses press the crown of glory: "ye shall receive a crown of glory" — the faithful shall be crowned; the crown of glory; the crowned; the the reward; the glorious; the the faithful. The expositors draw the lesson on the fadeth not away: "that fadeth not away" — never withers, unlike earthly garlands; the fadeth not away; the never withers; the unfading; the earthly garlands; the lasting. The witnesses observe the no earthly gain: the elder serves looking for no earthly gain; the no earthly gain; the serves; the the flock; the faithfully; the not gain. The expositors note the encouraging motive: the sure crown is the encouraging motive to faithful service; the encouraging motive; the the crown; the faithful service; the the reward; the sure. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the when the chief Shepherd shall appear, the ye shall receive a crown of glory that fadeth not away, and the crown promised. The verse thus records the crown promised to faithful pastors — teaching that the faithful elder shall receive an unfading crown of glory when Christ the Chief Shepherd appears.
5:
"Likewise, ye younger, submit yourselves unto the elder. Yea, all of you be subject one to another, and be clothed with humility: for God resisteth the proud, and giveth grace to the humble." The witnesses note the call to mutual submission and humility. Gill notes "ye younger" are not younger officers, but the younger members in age; a deference is to be paid by the younger to the elder, and a proper respect shown. The witnesses note Peter charging the younger to submit to the elder, all to be subject one to another and clothed with humility, since God resists the proud but gives grace to the humble; the call to humility, with God's favour on the humble and resistance to the proud — that Peter, having charged the elders, turns to the younger and to all: "ye younger, submit yourselves unto the elder" (the younger to yield respect and submission to their elders), "yea, all of you be subject one to another" (and all the members, mutually, in lowliness) "and be clothed with humility" (put on humility as a garment that covers and adorns the whole life) — and he grounds it in God's own dealing: "for God resisteth the proud, and giveth grace to the humble"; the witnesses note the weighty motive — God sets himself against the proud, opposing and abasing them, but pours grace upon the humble; so that humility is both commanded and crowned with God's favour, while pride is met by his resistance; God resisteth the proud, and giveth grace to the humble. The witnesses note ye younger, submit yourselves unto the elder... and be clothed with humility. The witnesses note for God resisteth the proud, and giveth grace to the humble.

The witnesses note the call to mutual submission and humility — clothed with humility as a garment; God resisting the proud but giving grace to the humble, so humility is commanded and crowned.

So the witnesses make the verse the call to humility under God's favour. The lesson the expositors draw is that all are to be clothed with humility, for God resists the proud but gives grace to the humble. The witnesses note the younger submit: "ye younger, submit yourselves unto the elder" — Gill notes the younger yield respect to the elder; the younger submit; the the elder; the respect; the submission; the deference. The expositors observe the subject one to another: "all of you be subject one to another" — all mutually, in lowliness; the subject one to another; the mutually; the all; the lowliness; the the members. The witnesses press the clothed with humility: "and be clothed with humility" — put on humility as a garment; the clothed with humility; the a garment; the put on; the covers; the adorns. The expositors draw the lesson on the resisteth the proud: "for God resisteth the proud" — God sets himself against the proud; the resisteth the proud; the against; the abasing; the opposing; the the proud. The witnesses observe the grace to the humble: "and giveth grace to the humble" — pours grace on the humble; the grace to the humble; the pours grace; the the humble; the favour; the crowned. The expositors note the commanded and crowned: humility is commanded and crowned with favour; the commanded and crowned; the humility; the favour; the pride resisted; the the motive. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the ye younger submit yourselves unto the elder and be clothed with humility, the for God resisteth the proud and giveth grace to the humble, and the call to humility. The verse thus records the call to humility under God's favour — teaching that all are to be clothed with humility, for God resists the proud but gives grace to the humble.
6:
"Humble yourselves therefore under the mighty hand of God, that he may exalt you in due time:" The witnesses note the call to humble submission under God's mighty hand. Gill notes "humble yourselves," or be humbled before God; quietly submit to his will, patiently bear every affliction without murmuring, be still under the rod, and stand in awe of God. The witnesses note Peter charging that they humble themselves under God's mighty hand, that he may exalt them in due time; the humble submission to God's hand that he may exalt in his own time — that Peter draws from God's dealing (v.5) the practical charge: "humble yourselves therefore under the mighty hand of God" (bow low and submit, especially under his afflicting, chastening hand, owning his sovereignty and your own unworthiness, not resisting or murmuring) — and he adds the gracious promise that crowns such humility: "that he may exalt you in due time"; the witnesses note the way up is down — those who humble themselves under God's hand he will raise and exalt, but "in due time" (in his own wise season, not before); so that the believer is to submit patiently to God's mighty, ordering hand, trusting that the lowly shall be lifted up when God sees fit; humble yourselves therefore under the mighty hand of God, that he may exalt you in due time. The witnesses note humble yourselves therefore under the mighty hand of God. The witnesses note that he may exalt you in due time.

The witnesses note the call to humble submission under God's mighty hand — that he may exalt in due time; the way up is down, the lowly lifted up when God sees fit.

So the witnesses make the verse the humble submission that God may exalt. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer humbles himself under God's hand, trusting that the lowly shall be exalted in God's own time. The witnesses note the humble yourselves: "humble yourselves therefore under the mighty hand of God" — Gill notes bow low and submit to God; the humble yourselves; the bow low; the submit; the to God; the his hand. The expositors observe the afflicting hand: especially under his afflicting, chastening hand; the afflicting hand; the chastening; the the rod; the patiently; the bear. The witnesses press the owning his sovereignty: owning his sovereignty and one's own unworthiness; the owning his sovereignty; the his sovereignty; the unworthiness; the not resisting; the not murmuring. The expositors draw the lesson on the exalt: "that he may exalt you" — that God may raise and exalt; the exalt; the raise; the lift up; the the lowly; the God. The witnesses observe the in due time: "in due time" — in God's own wise season; the in due time; the God's season; the not before; the his time; the wise. The expositors note the way up is down: the way up is down, the lowly lifted up; the way up is down; the the lowly; the lifted up; the humble; the exalted. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the humble yourselves therefore under the mighty hand of God, the that he may exalt you in due time, and the humble submission. The verse thus records the humble submission that God may exalt — teaching that the believer humbles himself under God's hand, trusting that the lowly shall be exalted in God's own time.
7:
"Casting all your care upon him; for he careth for you." The witnesses note the call to cast all care on God who cares for his own. Gill notes the words refer to "cast thy burden upon the Lord," which the Septuagint renders "cast thy care upon the Lord"; the care of the body and of all the affairs of life, about which saints should not be anxiously concerned. The witnesses note Peter charging that they cast all their care upon God, for he cares for them; the casting of all anxious care on the God who tenderly cares for his people — that Peter, in the same breath as humbling under God's hand, gives the sweetest relief from anxiety: "casting all your care upon him" (rolling the whole weight of every worry, burden, and anxious thought off your own heart and onto God) — and he gives the tender reason that makes this possible: "for he careth for you"; the witnesses note the comfort — the believer need not be crushed under the load of care, for he may cast it all, every care without exception, upon God, knowing that God himself cares for him with a Father's tender, watchful love; so that humble submission and freedom from anxiety go together, both resting on the loving care of God; casting all your care upon him; for he careth for you. The witnesses note casting all your care upon him. The witnesses note for he careth for you.

The witnesses note the casting of all anxious care on God — rolling every burden off the heart onto him; the believer freed from anxiety, knowing God cares for him with a Father's tender love.

So the witnesses make the verse the casting of all care on the caring God. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer may cast every anxious care upon God, who cares for him with a Father's love. The witnesses note the casting: "casting all your care upon him" — Gill notes rolling every worry onto God; the casting; the all your care; the rolling; the every worry; the onto God. The expositors observe the all care: every care without exception; the all care; the every care; the without exception; the the burden; the the anxiety. The witnesses press the off the heart: off your own heart onto God; the off the heart; the the weight; the onto God; the the burden; the relieved. The expositors draw the lesson on the he careth: "for he careth for you" — God cares for his own; the he careth; the God cares; the for you; the his own; the tender. The witnesses observe the a Father's love: God cares with a Father's tender, watchful love; the a Father's love; the tender; the watchful; the his people; the loving. The expositors note the freed from anxiety: the believer is freed from anxiety; the freed from anxiety; the not crushed; the the load; the cast; the relieved. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the casting all your care upon him, the for he careth for you, and the casting of all care. The verse thus records the casting of all care on the caring God — teaching that the believer may cast every anxious care upon God, who cares for him with a Father's love.
8:
"Be sober, be vigilant; because your adversary the devil, as a roaring lion, walketh about, seeking whom he may devour:" The witnesses note the call to sobriety and vigilance against the devil. Gill notes the apostle had exhorted to these before, but repeats them, sobriety and watchfulness being exceedingly necessary in the Christian life; for unless a man is sober in body and mind, he will not be watchful. The witnesses note Peter charging that they be sober and vigilant, because the devil, their adversary, walks about as a roaring lion seeking whom he may devour; the watchful sobriety needed against the prowling, devouring devil — that Peter, having spoken of casting care on God, warns that the believer is not therefore to be careless of his soul: "be sober, be vigilant" (self-controlled and watchful, awake and on guard) — and he gives the reason in a vivid and terrible image: "because your adversary the devil" (the great enemy and accuser of the saints) "as a roaring lion, walketh about, seeking whom he may devour"; the witnesses note the picture of the danger — the devil prowls like a hungry, roaring lion, restless and ravenous, ever seeking to destroy souls; so that the believer, though he casts his care on God, must yet keep sober and watchful, knowing so fierce and tireless an enemy is ever near; your adversary the devil, as a roaring lion, walketh about, seeking whom he may devour. The witnesses note be sober, be vigilant. The witnesses note your adversary the devil, as a roaring lion, walketh about, seeking whom he may devour.

The witnesses note the call to sobriety and vigilance against the devil — who prowls as a roaring lion seeking whom he may devour; the believer must keep watchful against so fierce an enemy.

So the witnesses make the verse the watchful sobriety against the devil. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer must be sober and vigilant, for the devil prowls like a roaring lion seeking to devour souls. The witnesses note the be sober: "be sober, be vigilant" — Gill notes self-controlled and watchful; the be sober; the be vigilant; the self-controlled; the watchful; the on guard. The expositors observe the necessary: sobriety and watchfulness are necessary in the Christian life; the necessary; the the Christian life; the sober; the watchful; the essential. The witnesses press the the adversary: "your adversary the devil" — the great enemy and accuser; the the adversary; the the devil; the the enemy; the the accuser; the the saints. The expositors draw the lesson on the roaring lion: "as a roaring lion, walketh about" — prowling like a hungry lion; the roaring lion; the prowling; the hungry; the restless; the ravenous. The witnesses observe the seeking to devour: "seeking whom he may devour" — ever seeking to destroy souls; the seeking to devour; the destroy; the souls; the seeking; the ravenous. The expositors note the keep watchful: the believer must keep sober and watchful against so fierce an enemy; the keep watchful; the sober; the so fierce; the the enemy; the ever near. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the be sober be vigilant, the your adversary the devil as a roaring lion walketh about seeking whom he may devour, and the watchful sobriety. The verse thus records the watchful sobriety against the devil — teaching that the believer must be sober and vigilant, for the devil prowls like a roaring lion seeking to devour souls.
9:
"Whom resist stedfast in the faith, knowing that the same afflictions are accomplished in your brethren that are in the world." The witnesses note the call to resist the devil, steadfast in faith. Gill notes "whom resist," by no means giving way to him, by indulging any sin or yielding to any temptation, but oppose him and stand against his wiles. Barnes notes that, feeble in themselves, they are to confide in God's aid. The witnesses note Peter charging that they resist the devil, steadfast in the faith, knowing the same afflictions are fulfilled in their brethren in the world; the steadfast resistance of the devil, with the comfort of shared suffering — that Peter, against so fierce a foe, gives the believer's defence: "whom resist stedfast in the faith" (do not flee or yield, but stand firm against him, and that "stedfast in the faith," the firm grip of faith being the strength by which the devil is withstood) — and he adds a strengthening comfort: "knowing that the same afflictions are accomplished in your brethren that are in the world"; the witnesses note the encouragement — the believer's sufferings and conflict with the devil are not strange or solitary, but the common lot of all the brethren throughout the world, who are enduring the very same trials; so that the saint resists the more steadfastly, knowing he does not suffer alone, but shares the warfare of the whole church; whom resist stedfast in the faith. The witnesses note whom resist stedfast in the faith. The witnesses note knowing that the same afflictions are accomplished in your brethren that are in the world.

The witnesses note the call to resist the devil steadfast in faith — strengthened by knowing the same afflictions befall the brethren in the world; the saint not suffering alone but sharing the church's warfare.

So the witnesses make the verse the steadfast resistance of the devil. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer resists the devil firm in faith, comforted that the same afflictions befall the whole brethren. The witnesses note the resist: "whom resist stedfast in the faith" — Gill notes stand firm, not yielding; the resist; the stand firm; the not yielding; the oppose; the the wiles. The expositors observe the stedfast in the faith: faith is the strength by which the devil is withstood; the stedfast in the faith; the faith; the the strength; the withstood; the firm grip. The witnesses press the not alone: Barnes notes, feeble in themselves, they confide in God's aid; the not alone; the feeble; the confide; the God's aid; the strength. The expositors draw the lesson on the same afflictions: "the same afflictions are accomplished in your brethren" — the common lot of all; the same afflictions; the common lot; the the brethren; the the same trials; the shared. The witnesses observe the in the world: "that are in the world" — throughout the whole church in the world; the in the world; the the whole church; the throughout; the the brethren; the everywhere. The expositors note the not solitary: the believer's conflict is not strange or solitary; the not solitary; the not strange; the the conflict; the shared; the the warfare. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the whom resist stedfast in the faith, the knowing that the same afflictions are accomplished in your brethren that are in the world, and the steadfast resistance. The verse thus records the steadfast resistance of the devil — teaching that the believer resists firm in faith, comforted that the same afflictions befall the whole brethren.
10:
"But the God of all grace, who hath called us unto his eternal glory by Christ Jesus, after that ye have suffered a while, make you perfect, stablish, strengthen, settle you." The witnesses note the prayer that the God of all grace would perfect and establish the sufferers. Gill notes "the God of all grace" has riches of grace in himself, has treasured up a fulness of grace in his Son, and is the author of all the blessings of grace and the giver of every grace of the Spirit. The witnesses note Peter praying that the God of all grace, who called them to eternal glory by Christ, would, after they have suffered a while, perfect, establish, strengthen, and settle them; the God of all grace perfecting and establishing the sufferers after a little suffering — that Peter ends his exhortations with a rich prayer for the suffering saints, addressing God by a glorious title and a sure ground of hope: "the God of all grace" (the fountain of every grace) "who hath called us unto his eternal glory by Christ Jesus" (whose calling is unto eternal glory, through Christ) — and he sets the brief trial against the eternal end: "after that ye have suffered a while" (the suffering being short, "a while" only) — and prays a fourfold blessing: "make you perfect" (fully fit and complete you), "stablish" (settle you firm), "strengthen" (give you might), "settle you" (ground you on a sure foundation); the witnesses note the comfort — the God of all grace himself will, after the brief suffering, do all this for his people, perfecting and establishing them in grace; make you perfect, stablish, strengthen, settle you. The witnesses note the God of all grace, who hath called us unto his eternal glory by Christ Jesus. The witnesses note after that ye have suffered a while, make you perfect, stablish, strengthen, settle you.

The witnesses note the prayer that the God of all grace, who called them to eternal glory, would after a little suffering perfect, establish, strengthen, and settle them; the brief trial against the eternal end.

So the witnesses make the verse the prayer that God perfect the sufferers after a while. The lesson the expositors draw is that the God of all grace will himself, after the brief suffering, perfect and establish his people. The witnesses note the God of all grace: "the God of all grace" — Gill notes the fountain of every grace; the God of all grace; the the fountain; the every grace; the riches; the in himself. The expositors observe the called to glory: "who hath called us unto his eternal glory by Christ Jesus" — called to eternal glory through Christ; the called to glory; the eternal glory; the by Christ; the the calling; the the end. The witnesses press the suffered a while: "after that ye have suffered a while" — the suffering short, a while only; the suffered a while; the short; the a while; the brief; the the trial. The expositors draw the lesson on the the fourfold: "make you perfect, stablish, strengthen, settle you" — a fourfold blessing; the the fourfold; the make perfect; the stablish; the strengthen; the settle. The witnesses observe the God himself: the God of all grace will himself do this; the God himself; the will do this; the his people; the after suffering; the perfect. The expositors note the establishing in grace: perfecting and establishing them in grace; the establishing in grace; the perfecting; the his people; the grace. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the after that ye have suffered a while make you perfect stablish strengthen settle you, and the prayer that God perfect. The verse thus records the prayer that God perfect the sufferers — teaching that the God of all grace will himself, after the brief suffering, perfect and establish his people.
11:
"To him be glory and dominion for ever and ever. Amen." The witnesses note the doxology ascribing glory and dominion to God. Gill notes glory is due to God for all the grace bestowed; the Syriac version connects the doxology with the God of grace who hath called us, ascribing to him glory, power, and honour for ever. The witnesses note Peter ascribing glory and dominion to God for ever and ever, sealed with Amen; the closing doxology to the God of all grace — that Peter, overflowing from the prayer just made, breaks into a doxology to the God of all grace who does such great things for his people: "to him be glory and dominion for ever and ever" — ascribing to God the "glory" (the honour and praise due to him for all his grace) and the "dominion" (the power and sovereign rule that are his) — and that not for a time but "for ever and ever," world without end; and he seals it with "Amen"; the witnesses note the fitness of the close — having shown the God of all grace as the believer's perfecter and establisher, Peter ends by giving him all the glory and owning his everlasting dominion, as is most due; to him be glory and dominion for ever and ever. Amen. The witnesses note to him be glory and dominion for ever and ever. The witnesses note Amen.

The witnesses note the closing doxology — to God be glory and dominion for ever; ascribing to the God of all grace the praise and sovereign rule that are his, world without end.

So the witnesses make the verse the doxology to the God of all grace. The lesson the expositors draw is that Peter ascribes to God all glory and everlasting dominion, as is most due to the God of all grace. The witnesses note the glory: "to him be glory" — Gill notes glory due to God for all his grace; the glory; the the praise; the the honour; the his grace; the due. The expositors observe the dominion: "and dominion" — the power and sovereign rule; the dominion; the the power; the sovereign rule; the his; the dominion. The witnesses press the for ever: "for ever and ever" — world without end; the for ever; the and ever; the without end; the everlasting; the eternal. The expositors draw the lesson on the Amen: "Amen" — sealed with Amen; the Amen; the sealed; the so be it; the close; the affirmed. The witnesses observe the the fitness: the fitness of the close, all glory to the God of grace; the the fitness; the all glory; the the God of grace; the the close; the due. The expositors note the everlasting dominion: owning his everlasting dominion; the everlasting dominion; the owning; the his rule; the for ever; the sovereign. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the to him be glory and dominion for ever and ever, the Amen, and the doxology. The verse thus records the doxology to the God of all grace — teaching that Peter ascribes to God all glory and everlasting dominion, as is most due.
12:
"By Silvanus, a faithful brother unto you, as I suppose, I have written briefly, exhorting, and testifying that this is the true grace of God wherein ye stand." The witnesses note the bearer of the letter and its great purpose. Gill notes Silvanus is the same with Silas, often mentioned in the Acts as a companion of Paul, whom Peter met in his travels and sent this letter by, or used as his amanuensis; his character is "a faithful brother." The witnesses note Peter saying that by Silvanus, a faithful brother, he has written briefly, exhorting and testifying that this is the true grace of God in which they stand; the bearer of the epistle and Peter's aim — to exhort and confirm them in the true grace — that Peter names the faithful messenger and sums up his whole purpose: he has written "by Silvanus, a faithful brother" (Silas, the trusted carrier or scribe of the letter), and "briefly" (a short letter, considering its weighty matter) — and his twofold design was "exhorting, and testifying that this is the true grace of God wherein ye stand"; the witnesses note the aim of the whole epistle — both to exhort them to their duties and to bear solemn witness that the doctrine and grace they had received and stood fast in was indeed "the true grace of God," the genuine gospel; so that Peter's closing word confirms them against all doubt, assuring them they stand in the real grace of God; testifying that this is the true grace of God wherein ye stand. The witnesses note by Silvanus, a faithful brother unto you, as I suppose, I have written briefly. The witnesses note testifying that this is the true grace of God wherein ye stand.

The witnesses note the bearer Silvanus and Peter's aim — exhorting and testifying that this is the true grace of God in which they stand; confirming them against doubt in the genuine gospel.

So the witnesses make the verse the bearer and the purpose of the letter. The lesson the expositors draw is that Peter wrote by faithful Silvanus to exhort them and confirm that they stand in the true grace of God. The witnesses note the Silvanus: "by Silvanus, a faithful brother" — Gill notes Silas, the trusted carrier; the Silvanus; the Silas; the the carrier; the the scribe; the faithful. The expositors observe the briefly: "I have written briefly" — a short letter for its weighty matter; the briefly; the short; the weighty matter; the the letter; the brief. The witnesses press the exhorting: "exhorting" — to exhort them to their duties; the exhorting; the their duties; the exhort; the the design; the urge. The expositors draw the lesson on the testifying: "and testifying that this is the true grace of God" — bearing witness to the true grace; the testifying; the witness; the the true grace; the the gospel; the genuine. The witnesses observe the wherein ye stand: "wherein ye stand" — the grace in which they stood fast; the wherein ye stand; the stood fast; the the grace; the received; the held. The expositors note the confirms against doubt: Peter confirms them against all doubt; the confirms against doubt; the against doubt; the assuring; the the real grace; the stand. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the by Silvanus a faithful brother unto you as I suppose I have written briefly, the testifying that this is the true grace of God wherein ye stand, and the bearer and purpose. The verse thus records the bearer and the purpose of the letter — teaching that Peter wrote by faithful Silvanus to exhort them and confirm they stand in the true grace of God.
13:
"The church that is at Babylon, elected together with you, saluteth you; and so doth Marcus my son." The witnesses note the greeting from the church at Babylon and from Mark. Gill notes the word "church" is supplied; some by "Babylon" understand Rome, so called figuratively in Revelation, an ancient opinion held by Papias; but whether Peter was at Rome when he wrote is uncertain — Babylon may be the literal city. The witnesses note Peter conveying the greeting of the church at Babylon, elected together with them, and of Mark his son; the salutation from a fellow-elect church and from Mark — that Peter sends the loving greetings of the believers where he was: "the church that is at Babylon, elected together with you, saluteth you" (the fellow-church, chosen of God as they were, sends its salutation) "and so doth Marcus my son"; the witnesses note the debate over "Babylon" — whether it means the literal city of Babylon (where a Jewish population dwelt, a natural place for the apostle to the circumcision) or, figuratively, Rome (as some ancients held, Rome being called Babylon in Revelation); and "Marcus my son" is John Mark, Peter's spiritual son or close companion; so that the letter closes with the warm fellowship of the churches and the bond of the gospel's family; the church that is at Babylon, elected together with you, saluteth you. The witnesses note the church that is at Babylon, elected together with you, saluteth you. The witnesses note and so doth Marcus my son.

The witnesses note the greeting from the church at Babylon, fellow-elect, and from Mark Peter's son — with debate whether Babylon means the literal city or, figuratively, Rome.

So the witnesses make the verse the salutation from a fellow-elect church and Mark. The lesson the expositors draw is that Peter conveys the loving greetings of the church where he was and of Mark, in the bond of the gospel's family. The witnesses note the church at Babylon: "the church that is at Babylon" — Gill notes the word church is supplied; the church at Babylon; the supplied; the the fellow-church; the where he was; the the believers. The expositors present the debate: the witnesses note the debate over "Babylon" — whether the literal city of Babylon (a natural place for the apostle to the circumcision) or, figuratively, Rome (as some ancients held). The witnesses press the elected together: "elected together with you" — chosen of God as they were; the elected together; the chosen; the fellow-elect; the of God; the as they. The expositors draw the lesson on the saluteth: "saluteth you" — sends its salutation; the saluteth; the salutation; the greeting; the loving; the sends. The witnesses observe the Marcus my son: "and so doth Marcus my son" — John Mark, Peter's spiritual son; the Marcus my son; the John Mark; the spiritual son; the companion; the close. The expositors note the the family: the warm fellowship of the gospel's family; the the family; the fellowship; the the churches; the the bond; the warm. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the church that is at Babylon elected together with you saluteth you, the and so doth Marcus my son, and the salutation. The verse thus records the salutation from a fellow-elect church and Mark — teaching that Peter conveys the loving greetings of the church where he was and of Mark.
14:
"Greet ye one another with a kiss of charity. Peace be with you all that are in Christ Jesus. Amen." The witnesses note the closing exhortation to mutual love and the benediction of peace. Gill notes the "kiss of charity" (or "holy kiss") is opposite to everything lascivious and impure, expressive of true love and affection, hearty and sincere. The witnesses note Peter charging that they greet one another with a kiss of charity, and closing with the benediction, "Peace be with you all that are in Christ Jesus. Amen"; the parting call to brotherly love and the blessing of peace on all in Christ — that Peter closes the epistle as he had filled it, with love and peace: "greet ye one another with a kiss of charity" (let the believers express their mutual love by the customary holy greeting, the token of sincere brotherly affection) — and he ends with the benediction: "Peace be with you all that are in Christ Jesus. Amen"; the witnesses note the fitness of the close — a letter written to suffering, scattered saints ends with the invoking of "peace," the deep peace of God, upon "all that are in Christ Jesus," every true believer united to him; so that Peter's last word to the persecuted church is the gift of peace in Christ, sealed with "Amen"; Peace be with you all that are in Christ Jesus. Amen. The witnesses note greet ye one another with a kiss of charity. The witnesses note Peace be with you all that are in Christ Jesus. Amen.

The witnesses note the parting call to brotherly love and the benediction of peace — the kiss of charity, and "Peace be with you all that are in Christ Jesus"; the gift of peace to the persecuted church.

So the witnesses make the verse the closing love and benediction of peace. The lesson the expositors draw is that Peter ends with the call to brotherly love and the invoking of peace on all who are in Christ. The witnesses note the kiss of charity: "greet ye one another with a kiss of charity" — Gill notes the token of sincere brotherly love; the kiss of charity; the the token; the brotherly love; the sincere; the affection. The expositors observe the not impure: opposite to everything lascivious, hearty and pure; the not impure; the not lascivious; the hearty; the sincere; the pure. The witnesses press the peace: "Peace be with you all" — the benediction of peace; the peace; the the benediction; the the blessing; the the gift; the closing. The expositors draw the lesson on the in Christ Jesus: "that are in Christ Jesus" — every true believer united to him; the in Christ Jesus; the every believer; the united; the in him; the true. The witnesses observe the to the persecuted: peace to the suffering, scattered saints; the to the persecuted; the suffering; the scattered; the the church; the peace. The expositors note the the gift of peace: Peter's last word is the gift of peace in Christ; the the gift of peace; the last word; the in Christ; the sealed; the Amen. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the greet ye one another with a kiss of charity, the Peace be with you all that are in Christ Jesus Amen, and the closing love and peace. The verse thus records the closing love and benediction of peace — teaching that Peter ends with the call to brotherly love and the invoking of peace on all who are in Christ.
